explanation: The input is simply the atomic proposition 'prop_0'. The equivalent LTL representation is just 'prop_0'.
The natural language statement is simply 'prop_0.' This corresponds directly to the atomic proposition 'prop_0' holding at the current moment in time. There are no temporal operators needed.
The natural language is just 'prop_0', which means we're simply referring to the atomic proposition prop_0 being true at the current time step.
The statement simply asserts that 'prop_0' holds at the current time. In LTL, to say a proposition holds now, we simply use the proposition itself.
The statement says that whenever 'prop_0' is true, then 'prop_1' must also be true at the same time. This is a standard safety property, which can be written as 'G (prop_0 -> prop_1)'.
The statement simply refers to the atomic proposition 'prop_0' being true now. In LTL, this is simply 'prop_0'.
The requirement is simply that 'prop_0' holds, without any temporal operator. So, the LTL formula is just the atomic proposition itself.

The specification is simply 'prop_0.', which means the atomic proposition 'prop_0' must hold now (at this point in time). The LTL formula expressing that 'prop_0' holds at the current state is just 'prop_0'.

The requirement 'prop_0 always prop_1' means that whenever prop_0 holds, at the same moment, prop_1 must also hold. In other words, it is always the case that if prop_0 is true, then prop_1 is also true.

The statement 'prop_0 always prop_1' means that whenever prop_0 holds, prop_1 must also hold, at all times. In LTL, this is written as 'G (prop_0 -> prop_1)', i.e., globally, if prop_0 is true, then prop_1 must be true.
